So I'm dealing right now with your page 24 through Page sticky fingers old fingers page 30 so this is a Version of a video seminar script This is for client of mine Essentially pitching get rich in real estate This version of the script has only Investor and financial newsletter subscribers Being driven to it. So there's actually eight versions of this script There is a unwashed masses version of the script which is pretty general and broad of course and form then the the the copy platform from which the financial newsletter subscriber version got built the Doctor version got built the CPA version got built Etc. So but the differences are more than just stick-in-word doctor If you had them all side by side you would see that there are some what significant changes all the way through The other thing for you with this script is you will find and I have identified them by hand in many cases Many of the things we talked about for an open you will see used in the open Many of the things we're about to talk about in the middle you will see in the middle So this is a current and fairly good example of the stuff. We're talking about applied And so by the way ain't a crying dog terrific, I mean it's just it's just terrific Yeah, and the lassie reference of course if you're of a certain age people would get the lassie reference but That film made a whole lot of people a whole lot of money So For example we talked about Short circuiting getting in a way of instant negative perceptions So the people being driven to this script do not yet know It's about real estate Okay, they have been driven here without that yet revealed to them. They are going to discover that So your third paragraph down is getting away from any other ideas They might have about what this is and again I boldface some things that Don't matter. They only matter for you So the anything risky like options or stock trading That's in here because of where these viewers are coming from They're coming from news financial newsletter subscriber lists investors business daily subscriber list Barron subscriber list Wall Street Journal book buyers. That's where they're coming from so the thing they see most is Options and stock trading and many will have a negative so we want to rule that out You will find A use of I'm still on your page 24 a use of the four-hour work week book as a reference There's no connection of course other than lending some credibility to the concept of Making a lot of money without putting in much time and Many will recognize the book title If they don't it's okay because I made sure to say New York Times best-selling book In front of it so even if they have never heard of the book They now instantly feel like they should have heard of the book and it has pretty much The same effect if you look at page 25 and you think about framing So this pitch because this one this version of it more than some of the others is going to people that are reasonably Affluent the pitch is framed as being about more than making money If you saw the unwashed masses version of this script, it's much more about This is a way you can get out of debt and this is a way you can have a better car Here this pitches frame more about lifestyle Has much or more as it is and again you'll see some of the references I bold-faced for you that are Script specific Only for the people being driven to this particular Webcast on page 26 the entire first paragraph Which I have bold-faced for you is only in this version of the eight different scripts We start establishing the who towards the bottom of page 26 What else do I want to call to your attention while you are here? Now if you jump to page 32 We go back in time to 1999 so this is the 1999 transcript of my Million-dollar-year speech at the success events and Again, I've marked through it What certain things are that match up with what we've talked about in open and what we'll talk about in middle and Also, if you compare it to the two current VSL scripts, I just gave you you will see that I haven't changed a whole hell of a lot in my approach to this between 1999 and and now So those are there for you To study At your leisure and we will move on to the middle So as we go from open to middle There are purposes for the middle and the first purpose for the middle is The one that a whole lot of people don't understand and There are less people understanding it less and less as time goes on so there's enormous pressure on marketers and presenters to abbreviate I I recently Just read a couple weeks ago. It's not a new book though. It's 2006 I think or 2008, but I read Bob Newhart's autobiography And Bob was talking there about how some of his most popular bits Abraham Lincoln versus Madison Avenue, which if you've never seen it you should find it on YouTube It's it's fabulous He's had to abbreviate in recent years because he's concerned about the attention span You know of the audience and I guess that's okay because Bob's not selling anything When he does the routine, but if he was selling anything when he did the routine my contention is he made the wrong choice I would be figuring out what to do with it Graphically pictorially video images behind me whatever in order to keep it its most effective length So one of the purposes of the middle is to keep the listener viewer long enough to turn interest into commitment So here's what happens about buying and Commitment the more invested in buying Person is the more likely they are to buy Because not buying is now disappointing frustrating and failure so Bill golf who's way back there in the corner of the room hiding so Bill had one of his preview seminars I didn't ask you about Pittsburgh. I will now so he had one of his preview seminars here the first the day before we started and He had a preview seminar the day before that in Pittsburgh. These are it's your business Craig It's same business except it's all state insurance agents so Bill brings a bunch of all state guys into a room and does how a day a day and then sells them into his coaching program So at the meeting here the slam dunk guy You would have to Figure out how not to sell flew here from Connecticut To be at the meeting the guy who drove eight miles across town He left without buying What's the difference they're both all state agents it's because for the guy to fly here from Connecticut To come to a free day and go home empty-handed That's a big disappointment So he's far more committed to buying If I lived at so in Washington DC outside of Washington, Virginia Where our other house is? It's in a mixed use You know little community So our condos or the condos are at the one end of the street and then there's a shopping village right there You walk out. There's a Hyatt at the other end and in between there's some stores and there's a Morton's and you know There's a new nose and there's a movie theater and there's all that stuff So for me to go for a walk from the condos and stop in four or five stores just to kill some time Well Carlos running you know go somewhere I Don't really I don't have any Emotional reaction to coming home from that without having bought anything If I get in the car and drive 25 minutes to a mall I'm coming home buying something Or I feel like I just What the hell did I go here for I mean I went through all this trouble. I got in my car I dodged them idiots in between me in the mall The woman texting who in the light turn green she sat there So I survived all that I found a parking place. I got in there Now I got to reverse that whole process to come home and I come home empty-handed makes no sense to me So I'm finding something to buy Even if what I thought I was good to buy in there So a couple weeks ago I go all the way down to the Barnes and Noble store I say all the way down to the Barnes and Noble store. It's like 20 minutes from here But you know you kind of go through the woods and it's It's a big to-do for me So I go all the way the Barnes and Noble store and the reason I'm going is because I'm good to get a History book and a travel book on the state of Oklahoma because I got a big copywriting job to do for Oklahoma So I go to Barnes and Noble to get these two books about Oklahoma. That's what I'm going for Turns out there are no travel books about taking a vacation and in Oklahoma. There's not there's a huge section of vacation books And there's not one Oklahoma in there. There's a Cleveland in there for Christ's sake and we're in Cleveland But there's no Oklahoma. Oh, I bought all those little Trump books that we gave away out there at the charity table Also had a big ass bag of trunk books All right, so I was I was not all that disappointed about not having my Oklahoma books now I hand it off to somebody and they'll go to Amazon or something and they'll find them But so the more committed somebody is so if they watch a tube So I'm gonna probably show you maybe before lunch if not after lunch Clips of one of these video sales letters. So the one we're testing right now is not much different from for that are working But it's trying to do a more difficult thing It's trying to bypass the preview seminar and Put people right into the paid weekend So it's an online video PowerPoint sales letter. It's an hour and 23 minutes long I Need an hour and 23 minutes to convince somebody to pay money and come to a weekend seminar without putting them in a preview so If we see bailouts now at 14 minutes in you will not see us reduce this thing to 13 and a half minutes You will see us keep working on what do we need to do to hold them long enough? That not Registuring is a disappointment That's one of the goals of the middle is to hold them long enough That not buying or whatever action it is that you want them to take feels like a disappointment Notice so Zappos is such an interesting thing. I don't know how the hell they make any money, but it's deal there So last summer word of vacation home and my stepdaughter is of the age that She would use that pose. I would never use that pose of course for a variety of reasons, but so Here comes the FedEx truck and She comes lunging in 12 boxes shoe boxes. I said what the hell are you doing? She said I need a pair of flip flops I said Jennifer There's a Target store They got flip flops for crazy. Yeah, but I called Zappos. I got flip flops I said all this. Oh, no, I'm gonna try them all on and see which one I like and then FedEx picks everything up It takes it back tomorrow I'm like appalled right Okay, now the best part of this story is she didn't like any of them And was bitterly disappointed Because think of the process now actually if she had just run down to the corner of the Target store and not found any She liked she would have come home. She wouldn't have been that frustrated But this like ruined her whole day Because think at a commitment level she had to go to the Zappos site She had to pick out 12 different things she might like she had to order it which I don't know much about that But I I watched Bill Teere trying to buy me a chair once for Christmas on a website That's my total experience with this. I could have built the chair fast. I Mean she got her done once and it crashed and she did it again It wouldn't accept her code by then I just you know I left but I'm standing behind her I'm thinking there's an office back store right over here. We could just go over there get a chair And we don't have to do this But so just like ruined her whole day But think of the commitment level that was involved in this and the expectation level of what was going to happen that's the chief purpose of the middle of A presentation is to get to the point that not buying is a disappointment For the person who doesn't buy Not I'll tell you if you get nothing else for your time here today, and you really get that you got something valuable because most people don't understand it and Everybody you have do things for you Particularly video and online who are the technical people who know how to do this. They don't know this at all This is not anything they are trying to accomplish They are trying to convey your message as quickly as possible Is really what they're trying to do and even if you can convey it quickly You don't want to because of this You need a level of commitment that walking away empty-handed from it is a disappointment So if you watch our GK I see like launch pattern which David Darcy spearhead brilliantly Dave was here with us the last two days. He left this morning I don't know cuz flights or whatever and I think he's he looks like he's one step away from dad. I think he's pretty tired They are they are riding those two pretty hard and putting them up wet every night in the stall But but date I mean they do a fantastic job, but if you pay attention There's sort of a system now that works for us And it's usually three or four run-up videos That are 15 or 20 minutes in and of themselves Getting to a three or four hour live webcast And I with formed commercials in it just like you would do with an infomercial so if you pay it So if we're on live every 50 so and it's like 15 Okay, so there's 50 minutes alive and then there's a 10 to 12 minute canned Commercial call to action then there's 50 minutes alive and then there's a 10 to 12 canned commercial call to action Guess who all the buyers are the ones who went through all the run-up videos and have sat through the entire webcast in many cases a Third of the sales occur in the last Segment of the webcast and they've been there the whole time Now if you tell the average video person that you're going to make people sit through six hours of video In order to buy online They will throw up all over you Okay, but that's exactly what we've done and we scheme to do all the time We don't need six hours to pitch a 297 dollar how to get more referrals box I can Competently pitch it to you in 30 or 45 minutes But the commitment level for this person who has now come three times to the web room To see the run-up videos come back a fourth time made sure they had food and water So they didn't have to leave sat there and sat through the commercial three times and come back It's a huge flop for them To walk away empty-handed. So I'm using the middle to create commitment Another reason for middle is to create emotional attachment to you and or to what is being offered So you can rationally explain something quick You need more time to develop emotional attachment I'm Emotionally motivated to do this. I Like and respond and trust and bond with the person who is talking to me about it The middle job is to raise and overcome doubts and objections All group presentations all presentations by media the major handicap They have versus one-to-one selling is in one-to-one selling I adjust based on your questions and objections can't do that in group You can do it in a room with a small group if you're really highly skilled because it's very dangerous You can adjust to a small group maybe by what you see Body language in a group sales environment like QVC or HSN The numbers are telling you whether what you are saying is being effective or not and you can adjust But most of the time we don't have an ability to adjust that means you have to raise and overcome every possible objection doubt and question that if we did it Individually with if 200 of you are going to watch it and but I was going to sit and sell to each one of you one-on-one And there were 42 different questions and objections The group presentation has to handle 42 different questions and objections now some are alike enough Just voiced differently that we can clump them So maybe the 42 would come down to 16 but still that's a lot and generally there are a lot of reasons not to buy And they've got to be handled in the context of a group presentation Next a purpose of a middle is To get sequential agreement So that the person can buy so here's the way you have to think through this Buying is not about okay. It's time to buy this you want to buy it or not want to buy it There's a series of things a person has to agree to Before unless they're insane they can buy this thing so the price and the superiority of the clock Say we got to be all the way back at You need to be able to tell time You need to know what time it is. That's like the first agreement then you need a Thing to tell you what time it is and so you know the wristwatch business is in real trouble You know the high end because people don't buy watches to tell time with when they buy a $25,000 watch They buy it to collect it or they buy it to wear it and impress you or whatever but you know the hundred dollar watch businesses el scrued oh right now because Look at the risks in the room Less and less and less people wear watches because their phone tells them what time it is All right, so somehow if we're going to sell hundred dollar watches, we got to convince you that You're going to have to agree with me that Telling time by your phone is not as good as telling time by your watch All right, otherwise don't matter what watch I show you what price I show you don't make any difference So everything has this this series of sequential agreements That you have to sort of say and you'll see speakers Mostly clumsily, but in some environments it works. You'll see them do that with trial closes all the way along Again Done video. We don't have the ability necessarily to do it and sometimes it's clumsy, but you got to cover it So here's your example for the fire There's a whole series of agreements that have to occur Before I Can get eight grand out of you for a fire alarm And so you have to agree to this and you have to agree to this and you have to agree to this and you have to agree to this And then finally you're ready and able to agree to oh, yeah I need them and I need them hung tonight because we could burn to death tonight By the way, that was always a problem in that business because in order to buy him Ultimately you have to agree that at any moment Your house could burst into flames and then because these damn things have to be installed Having the installer come next Thursday Now negates the whole so that industry had to figure that out The really great sales guys who ran two appointments and I had an installer with them Guy sat in the car until the sale was closed and then he came in and installed the alarms The cheap worst ones at least learned how to install enough that they could temporarily hang to and send the installer the next day But these sequential agreements have to occur So I've given you I've taken you through 16 points Actually to get in that example to get to the sequential agreement Another purpose of the middle is to create this emotional attachment to you and or to what is being offered So sort of what has to be there? So in most cases not all because sometimes Desire can be inflamed to such a point that trust is not necessary But it's a difficult way to sell things in personal power one So we for many years I assume they still do it Guthearen not only focus group Before in production, but once something's working they would focus group buyers And really what we're looking for is of course why you bought Because you may have bought for reasons we think are three on the list of reasons people buy And it may turn out that's a 10 and what we thought was a 10 so in personal power one One of the things so if you remember the first Tony show at all There's a castle in it and there's getting off the private jet in it and there's you know the symbols of wealth and success The personal power one the focus group of the buyers now keep in mind This is of the buyers the overwhelming majority didn't believe the castle was his they didn't believe the plane was his and They assumed that all the testimonials were paid And they bought anyway Now however most pitch people are not as good on camera has was Tony even then and The desire for the promise was so great that they bought Anyway, so you can pull it off, but it's not easy It is easier if a level of trust occurs in the person who is pitching So one kind of emotional attachment is trust another is desire for association This is my guy So on the next page if I show you this It's an old bumble bumper sticker it says work harder millions on wealth welfare depend on you okay now So if I show you if I show that to people they have a reaction to it Okay, so most of you your reaction is sort of positive Right, so the reaction is yep. You're my guy. You know, we're we're Sympatic old copacetic here great now. I want to associate myself with you other people have the opposite reaction You know, I want to get as far away from this moron as I possibly can and we should take away his right to vote And then there's you know reactions in between there's the sort of people who like tolerate me for the other benefits But then immediately oh no here he goes again, you know There's some people like have a the equivalent of the drinking game, you know They stick count during time with me how many times I you know make a political reference they don't like guys But people have a reaction right so Again, it helps if you know in advance What kind of reaction? The people you're presenting to will have to certain stimuli Because that allows you to use stimuli that they will respond to positively and make them Want to associate with you and then there's sort of a checklist of the e factors That you want to try and hit so this is a way to score The middle of a presentation is to see how many of these 10 e factors you've hit if you go through the Sequential agreement of the fire alarm example you will find numbers 1 3 5 6 7 8 10 So the score is 7 out of 10. It gets a 70 percent score If it only had three of them it would have a 30 percent score So one is love sex acceptance respect self-respect some version of that To his popularity social status. I want to be like him. He's cool Three is intelligence. I'm smarter because I buy this. I'm in the know because I buy this My friends will be amazed because I know this for his greed wealth power autonomy 5 is security safety Protection 6 is guilt shame possibility of regret versus peace of mind 7 is pride ability achievement admiration the envy of others more people by the way want envy than they want admiration 8 is goodness honesty respect because I do this because I buy this. I'm a good person 9 significance reason for being because I buy this I'm a more significant person This is a meaningful part of life 10 is fear of ill health age or death versus immortality So those are your 10 most useful e factors and you can score the middle of your presentation based on how many of them you have in the presentation If you happen to be a Freelancer by the way, this is this is helpful in beating controls. So if somebody has let's say a 25-minute web video that works or They have a group presentation that works So right now I'm fixing the in the room pre well Improving the in the room Presentation from this get rich in real estate company of their previous speakers. So one of the things I do is take the transcript and score it I got ten different color highlighters I go through in a highlighter to match up with my ten and I'm not good enough to do all ten of them All in one fell swoop So I do like two and then I go back to the beginning and look for the next two And all the but Lee it's going to get a score and the way to improve it Is to put the missing ones in? So if you happen to be trying to improve an existent control, this is this is a useful Tool for you, but you should know by the way Speakers particularly but hardly anybody is this Procedural about what they do with group presentations There's a lot of web video that there's no thinking process about this at all You know, but just jumps in front of the camera and off we go in part because it's cheap. It's free I can just stand a camera up and do it and we can broadcast it for nothing And we drive everybody to it with email for nothing So it gets treated that way when you spend a half a million dollars producing an infomercial You're going to spend another quarter of a million to test it everybody's attitude kind of changes about that Okay, that's why so so Brian Kurtz for example says that You know, it's fine that you're making a lot of money online But if you want to really learn something pay attention to the people that have to buy printing and postage Because everybody's a lot cash more casual when they don't have to write a check to the printer And they don't have to write a check to the United States Postal Service All they have to do is push a button and send it out as email When you're going to go when you're going to mail what did what did Malin check? Are you in a room? I think he said for each of his boot camps. He's dropping 35,000 pieces of mail Well, when you're going to go write a check for 70 grand you kind of think about it When you're just going to slap together an email and drive people to a sales letter You slap together and stuck online and it really you're nobody's writing a check you treat it differently. So this is pretty Technical I guess but it is a way to analyze a presentation backwards as well as to engineer one forward Also in the middle, it's where we do our explanation What are the things they? Have to know in order to buy they may not necessarily drive the sale But if they're omitted Then they hurt the sale so in reality if If Ben's going to tell them how to triple the size of their law practice in 90 days with no work and no brains It almost doesn't matter what the media is But it will kill the sale if they don't know what the media is So it's a series of three webinars and two Q&A calls and you know that so that's necessary explanation If you sell health It's necessary to have for most products. It's necessary to have Some appeasing explanation of why and how it works So you won't see a proactive pitch None for the three-step system and I don't even think you see it for the new one-step Glop, which I didn't work on you won't see a proactive pitch that excludes Some version of the little drawing of the skin and the little dudes down into the skin and how this glop Penetrates deep into the skin and that's why it prevents breakouts. It doesn't just cure breakouts now. It's not much of an explanation It's not candidly even a scientifically clinically accurate explanation, but it is an explanation Enough for the person to go. Okay. I get why that works All right, so we do that in the middle The middle is where we prove See you were out of the room. I referenced you and it did no good because nobody saw you So So the middle is where we prove our case and This is all the ways that we tend we prove it with science or junk science or fake science And one's as good as the other We prove it with You know the best fake science still yet of a big big big big big successful marketing thing was sharks don't get cancer To sell shark cartilage. That's like the best fake science ever Okay So because sharks don't get cancer, which is a fact they don't you should eat shark cartilage Implication being you won't get cancer either It's just as dumb as because sharks don't get cancer if you go to the aquarium every day and watch them You won't get cancer one one has nothing to do with the other but People go. Oh, well that you know make sense You may have noticed that The horse that won the derby and the pretense and they didn't win the Belmont his breathing problems And you can only give him so much Waysics and then you aren't allowed to give him anymore So the thing was racing and they had to get special permission in New York to let him race with it in the Belmont with a big No strip on his nose, you know like the ones you buy in a drug store that you stick on and they expand your nostrils So you don't snore during the night, you know that product so the horse race with a big ass version of this stuck on his nose and These things have come and gone in racing at least three times about five years ago They made the rounds through harness racing and everybody had them and you'd be in a race driving and these things are flying off The horses noses and hitting you in the head as they went on by Just like they come off you in the you know in the middle of the night, of course, this is all nonsense Horses breathing systems are entirely different than human breathing systems. I mean this does nothing I mean you might as well tie a lucky shamrock to his ass Yeah, because that's what that's what this is. This is superstition is what this is, but there's a Scientific explanation and you know so there's science. There's authority borrowed authority fake authority. There's celebrity social peer-proof One-on-one or in aggregate still usually is the best Demonstration is also extremely powerful and something that we often leave out Many of us that we don't need to leave out and I will jump to it and we'll Get the demonstration before Shortly before lunch no matter what because you're good at see some demonstration video When you come back from lunch So we prove our case the technical mechanical process here by the way is now to make a list of every assertion So if you're working backwards Take your script take your PowerPoint slides take your presentation that you're already using and Either you or somebody capable goes through it and makes a list of every assertion Now you may be able to go back and cross some out that you are convinced our common knowledge So, you know if your product in part works because there's gravity You ought to have it on the list Now you may ultimately okay. I'll cross that one out everybody kind of However if your product works because of evolution The statistics tell us you might want to have to deal with that one. I Think the last study Where's is Nelson here Nelson are you here? Did you go home? Do you do well they do it was a How many people in Congress there was a thing in the news not too long ago about the number of people in Congress Who do not believe in evolution they believe in that you remember what the? Yeah, I mean it's a significant number Right and in the last the last election I think on the Republican side It was all of the candidates in the primary except for two So if you happen to think that's an accepted fact And somehow your pitch is based on everybody accepting the fact You're wrong right so you make a list of every assertion and then you consider do we have to prove it? and you better than prove Everyone that you are going to make So that's that's the technique. That's how you do that in the middle represent our human interest stories so if you have if You or whoever it is that you're building this for who is pitching if you have a origin story a reason Why story a turning point in life story we put that in the middle If great before and afters go in the middle The be like me appeal goes in the middle the superstar testimonials so not all testimonials are equal Most good presentations use a number of testimonials, but you may want to feature one or two More than you feature the others Sometimes that may be somebody with a severe handicap Who is able to succeed at whatever it is that you're pitching or use whatever it is that you are? selling so In get rich in real estate if we can find somebody with no legs and no arms who does the contracts by the pencil held in his teeth It makes a million dollars a year I would rather use that person than somebody with two legs and two arms Because people get the idea well a legless armless guy can do it. I guess I don't have much of an excuse Because I could actually walk around and I can text people so You're looking for the poster boy or girl kind of testimonial to Accentuate during the course of a so we did this We did a Nancy Kwan Most of you well many of you will be familiar with the Nancy Kwan pearl cream spots But there was a Nancy Kwan infomercial for a Oriental Beauty Secret skin care system a whole box of galop rather than one, you know thing and Stan Jacobs and I Spent a week finding the testimonials and then another week taping the testimonials and we found Three generations women three generations all using the galop. So we found and they were in West Virginia, I think So there's a There's a grandma there's a ma and there's a daughter and they're all using the galop It's great human interest story. It's horrific. So they go on the list we fly them in And we go to interview them and of course we start with grandma. So grandma is 209 years old and She's shrunken So she sort of looks like a dr. Ruth kind of character and She's got jowls. I mean she's got layers of skin, you know like a like a beagle dog Big old beagle dog, you know, Ron Ron White says he's got one of them dogs and at night sometimes he Inside the flaps of skin. He puts some M&M. So when the dog wakes up in the morning, he kind of finds him and he's excited. So We we could have done the same thing with this woman we could have stuffed her full of M&Ms, you know and center on her What so we're gonna show her now in a skincare commercial, you know, Stan and I were going, I don't know, you know Mama looked okay, but grandma, you know and the kid looked fine So ultimately we decided to do it anyway because it was such a great Human interest story. I'm on this product then I got my daughter on this product and her daughter's on this product It's better than I'm on this product and look how great I look this actually was a more interesting Presentation just as an aside the old woman's telling me because they're all in continuity You know this stuff's coming every month the credit cards getting whacked and she says, you know I've my whole basement is almost full of this now because it keeps I give it away to friends I use less and less of it because my face is getting smaller and I don't go out that much And I yeah, it's all I could do not to say hon. You can call up and stop But you know my client is the one shipping this so But so you're looking for the star Testimonial and then you want to give them a little more time in your presentation than Democritizing it you may want them with you live and the other testimonials just shown on tape You may want to give a whole segment in multi segments to that one Testimonial that is either on set with you live that is in the room with you live or Or for their video you take a really good crew and you go and you tape them in their natural habitat With their dogs and kids and boats and houses Everybody else gets taped in a hotel room up against a background with a potted palm in a bookcase You you make undemocratic decisions about this Including any amount of time in the middle We answer and invalidate the doubts and objections okay, and So all this then falls into a some sort of structure For the time frame or you're gonna have two hours on stage or you're gonna have 90 minutes on stage You get a 45 minutes on stage or they got to watch two hours, so they got to watch an hour Or they got to watch a half Okay, I will almost always divide the middle into either three or four Depending upon the length of the middle so there are either three or four defined segments that then these pieces get fit into and There's timing for this like Darcy immediately knew these are structured 50 10 to 12 50 10 to 12 50 10 to 12 The 28 and a half minute infomercial is usually structured Long medium short commercials in between But sometimes others the special middle elements and I Didn't find there's one of these I meant to find an example for you and I didn't But you will be able to go find them on your own if you like so special middle elements So one is as I just said is there anybody that there's a reason for you to have video of them in their head Habitat so most people shoot all the testimonials at one time They shoot them all in one place Often they don't even bother to move around To the four different locations in the hotel room that they might be able to use they don't build sets for them So now in your presentation when we see the testimonials, it's kind of evident They were all shot at one time in one place Some people are uptight in any plan or less uptight in their natural environment Right one way some people cheat when they shoot them all in one places. They come real tight So there's no background at all. However, particularly in HD a lot of people don't look so good real tight It definitely doesn't work for skincare testimonials It's just you know, not a good thing So you have to think about whether it there's a reason and it's worth the money to go get this piece of proof That we're going to use in our presentations in their world rather than in a studio with a green screen or in a hotel room outside of seminar and And And so that's one middle element to think about is whether or not you're going to pick one or more to Present in a more interesting way than just what they are saying Because if you back up now and you think about each isolated thing in a group presentation So if I were selling and I am now going to cut to showing you three Video testimonials one right after the other we are back to the open of a thing and When I open if it is really not compelling You will mentally tune out So it's more compelling if I open with my testimonial climbing out of his airplane Or Standing on his boat holding the giant fish he just caught then if I have him sitting on a stool up against a yellow wall with a Palm tree next to him Now it's much more cumbersome to go shoot him on his boat with the fish It's a pain in the ass Because he will drop the fish the fish will bite him the boat will rock and the camera will fall over Etc. Etc. Etc. But it could be worth it unique physical locations So of all the Dave Deldato get rich real estate infomercials you can go back and see them all If you like they're probably all on YouTube or you can get them from Jordan Whitney if you if that happens to interest you He shot three with John Davidson as the host if you don't know who John Davidson is Google them At the time most people knew who John Davidson was the most successful show was a panel of Dave's superstar testimonials on high stools on the beach in Hawaii Mostly in Hawaii shirts With the ocean behind them at a hotel with John Davidson interviewing them on the beach Doesn't really have anything to do with anything But when you clicked into it, it was more visually interesting than In a four-walled room. So unique physical locations, there's sometimes a reason for those Then we have The garden party scene So you don't see it a lot in web video, but you see it in almost every infomercial That has been successful particularly those aimed at women consumers But you'll see it in men shows too. So we call it the garden party scene so the garden party scene it doesn't sometimes it's out so in in In proactive and in Victoria principal There's a few times when it is actually like out on a patio in the garden More often than not you will see this in the living room, but the scene has four or five or six the garden party scene in the in the Vic show has 20 I think because they're sitting at round tables on the patio if it's done in a living room You're kind of bound by a couch and a few chairs without it looking now like you're not in a real living room So there will be four or five the celebrity will be there The host will be there if there's a host different than the celebrity and they are all sitting around chatting About the product and their use of it and the benefits and there it is a Loose people talk over each other back and forth kind of conversation the reason you see it more in shows aimed at women is because a guys don't do this So for the boat you put guys in a living room and in five hours. There's like four words spoken and three of those you can't put on television so So you don't see it as much in guy shows, but you see it a lot in women shows now to get The five the four minute clip that you might use in your infomercial in your webcast in your DVD However, you're going to use it if I was going to cut to it here in part of a live presentation You may shoot three hours, and you're going to have some post because you really do want them to forget That they are doing anything but chatting But it's an extremely powerful device because the viewer sort of steps into the room with them and They have a conversational experience that doesn't feel like it was scripted and structured to advance the sale Which it wasn't that was done in post-production which the way you edited it So it's a very powerful tool the garden party scene the man on the street deal So the man on the street again is used all the time been used throughout history Joe Sugarman's blue blocker show the first three successful shows were all man on the street Try the glasses on what do you think stop at people on the street a more recent orthotics infomercial for things? You put in your shoe They took the truck to the shopping mall and they stop at people on the street And they're putting the thing in their shoe, and they're all reacting. There's a fitness show that was done that way I think it was one of the body by Jake shows. I'm not Sure, but you see it done a lot some apparently spontaneous involvement of the public in a verifying demonstration right and again The infomercial industry lives and lived on this people doing video for the web less People doing video clips to show in a live presentation less, but no good reason It works it works. It works. It works So you'll see the man on the street stuff all the time again if you use YouTube I assure you you can find any of these infomercial examples and it is about a verifying demonstration So I'm in pitchman The piece I'm going to show you is not this if you haven't seen this show by the way there this predate shark tank You can go buy it on DVD I'm just gonna show you one little clip today But pitchman was about people coming to them with products and then finally picking something and then doing it and so For the same product. I'm gonna show you a clip. They would be more credible authority figures for a foot insole than a bunch of fire guys Well, think about it. How interested is anybody in podiatrists? Hey, we're gonna see a room full of firefighter guys. Well, that's inherently more exciting and Interesting so if you're gonna choose between the two you want firefighters, right? So there's this man on the street thing. There's the live audience thing some version of a live I can't day Some version of a live Response from an audience again tons of infomercials have done this way have been still are in some cases It's a real audience Some cases. It's a rented audience If you shoot in LA you can spec up there's companies that just deliver you the audience You build a seating chart and you want a fat black woman right there and you want an Asian guy right there and you want a Much better looking but older guy than you right there and you want a married couple here They go get them and put them in place and you get exactly what you spec the live audience The key is that they are actually involved not just sitting there So at some point they are asking questions And getting answers they are five of them are being picked and coming up and participating in a demonstration So one of the demonstration clips I'm going to show you is demonstrating an intangible product an IP product like many of us Rather than a tangible product doing it with a live audience and doing it with live audience part participation So all that's in the middle Because the average American now has the attention span of a ferret after three hours at Starbucks We have to work at holding their interest for long presentations So I'm on your page 50 just for the record So The first thing to know about holding interest in long presentations in a short attention span world is Get the best possible audience you can so This is consistently the part of of Selling one to many that so many people forget is that the effectiveness even of the most effective presenter in Media or in person is Largely it's heavily influenced by things that presenter has can't control By his presenting and that the presentation can't affect so you could put Tony in such a bad place that he could not be effective You could put the best one with the best presentation in front of a really bad audience and You will have Low effectiveness there is not a professional speaker in the country alive or dead who hasn't Sometimes it's obvious why other times for example, I can't sell anything in Tucson So I don't know why that is but I have established with sufficient number of attempts That I cannot make money in Tucson, Arizona It's I just can't do it no matter what I do in Tucson Everybody walks out of the room and nobody buys So after six or eight of these experiences over a decade I haven't been back to Tucson in I don't know 30 years And I have no interest in going there and I would never go there on vacation and give the bastards money because they will not They will not give me anybody. So Tucson is out, you know So I have no explanation for that Right, I can tell you why I can't do well in Vermont That I have a answer for you for right because it's been in Jerry land You know and even if I don't say anything they sense from me that We profoundly disagree over pretty much everything in life You know I flew I Flew from Phoenix to Seattle for a Peter Loegig with and I get him confused either Ben or Jerry sitting next to me I don't remember which one and and And the flight got canceled three times and they had to bring an additional plane So we were in Sky Harbor Airport in Phoenix for a while before we even got on the plane. So, you know, we conversed and And Four hours four and a half hours We're landing in Seattle and whichever one of them was better. Jerry says to me, you know, I haven't asked you Do you like ice cream? I said, yeah, everybody likes ice cream. He's great We finally found the one thing in four and a half hours about which we have any agreement So, you know, so I mean sometimes there's a reason sometimes you don't know the reason but look Who you put this presenter and presentation in front of matters a lot and You got to give thought to Who you invest money or time in driving to see it? See the worst thing about infomercials is you really can't target audience much It's the toughest thing about there. Where's on the web see you can Pick and choose who you drive to go watch it when you put them in a room you can pick and choose So, you know for four years we did preview seminars for chiropractors in Dennis mostly Well, I had one speaker on the road three weeks out of every four for a couple of years I went out for one week out of the ones we're putting docks in the room three hours a night And we're selling them stuff. We learned as we went along First of all, there was some geography. We never went back to you did it once and you never went there again Because compared to all the other geographies that the response was horrible I mean downtown New York Manhattan very tough to make work. I think in Four and a half years. We did it twice even though you got great density and you got all that but you know the doc The one I did I remember Vividly the doc comes in he's got four staff people with him He sits the four staff people in the back. He sits right in the front He puts his feet up on the table and he reads the news people I'm not selling him You know he has come predetermined that he is good to Verify his conclusion, which is it's all bullshit It won't work in New York and you know and he's good to go home. So but we also over time we quickly learned that Women with hyphenated names so doctors with hyphenated names didn't buy We didn't do very well with Asians anywhere except in California And so you stop mailing them and Gradually we were putting more and more in the rooms who were well matched with the message and the Presenter and fewer and fewer who weren't matched And so we got by the end of six months with foster on the road. We were at a 95% close rate Every night now some of that was him because he was really good But some of it was what we did about if you will rigging the audience in front of him And so you have to think about rigging the audience them then links the most relevant message possible so if I take the generic great unwashed masses vs. L script for Get rich in real estate And I drive the investor business daily crowd to it and I drive home service business owners carpet cleaning Pest control gutter repair and I drive them to the same vs. L Yes, some of each will buy But not nearly as many as if I drive the investor Business daily crowd to one video sales letter and I drive the home service guys to another one because I can make the message relevant to them and Why wouldn't you how hard is it to do five instead of one? It's not five times harder, right? But you'll get much more effectiveness. So the most relevant message possible You have to hold interest. So another thing to think about is not one long continuous presentation with a Pitch at the end In many cases you can get away with that in a physical room Particularly if they have come there to be with you or they have paid money to be with you You can use a one-act play structure We start we go we pitch We finish But in almost any other media it's very hard to hold people's interest for so Our presentation in a success track years was three hours long, right? So we put them in a room We held them for three hours When you sent that to them on a CD and on a DVD it didn't work very well Because They don't have to stay so now it had to be cut up into pieces with the pitch in there more than once just like we now do on the live Webcast so if you did the webcast For four hours like you do and in the room Dave I whoever else is on camera Lee we would all start and we would go for three hours and 40 minutes and then we would pitch and That would not work nearly as well Has we might be able to get away with it in a room so most of these presentations get divided into three act or four act plays and The first act is mostly the open The second or third act is middle the fourth act is end But there's buying opportunities at least between the acts if not during the acts In the infomercial world all three Cover the territory needed to make a buying decision Because they click in and out Periodic cliffhangers Teases and new headlines So whenever there's a shift from one act to the other When there's a go-to commercial and we're coming back from commercial When we know from statistics that we lose a lot 18 minutes in at these points You now need cliffhangers and teases you've got to go back and write three promises of what's to come You've got to tell people what's going to happen next Even as you are telling them something Currently so that you hold on to them for a few more minutes In the infomercial world again, we grow up thinking about One more minute one more minute one more minute. What do we have to do to hold this person for one more minute? Hal had a show one year that just Sort of sagged right around 15 16 minutes in and you knew people were like bailing out of this Left and right and how did cheap shows he did fast shows he didn't do a lot of money in post and Remember it was it was a subliminal tape pitch and the presenter was pretty good, but it just you know So he wanted to do something really cheap to fix it so he had two girls in bikinis just walk across the set Behind a guy real slow they walked on they walked across the set they walked off nobody said anything about them They had nothing to do with the show nobody nobody referenced anything Nothing right, but when you were sitting there watching it you were about ready to And then you stayed for another couple of minutes, I'm not sure I Recommend the technique, but I am suggesting to you. There's a time of place to do mixed content Presentations it's very hard to sell with an information presentation only It's even hard just to hold people's attention who paid for an information presentation only Very hard. You need a mix you need stories. You need humor or you need demonstration You need something going on you know joke about you know Joke about movies, you know car level occasionally want me to go to some chick flick thing I'll say is there a car crash in the first 15 minutes. No, I don't think so fine you go You know, I mean I want like something to happen. I don't want a two-hour meaning of life conversation That is why we got divorced in the first place is two-hour meaning of life conversations Let's not go watch people have a two-hour meaning of life conversation Mixed media mixed media right photos cartoons video Text I'll get rich in real estate stuff We get away with a lot big lengths of PowerPoint and voice Really a sales letter stuck up on the web But the longer they get the more we need to do longer ones the more we are using cartoons and graphics and video and Changing type fonts and kind of all the stuff you would do if you could in print There has to be enough speed and pace That they have to work just a little at keeping up And it can't be a Steady pace all the way through so pace Matters you can't be leaving them behind But they can't be ahead of you either You've all sat in life performances where that's the case or watch the TV show or a movie where that's the case You are two sentences ahead of them Right and you now know what they're gonna say and you're saying it in your head Before so you remember CSI Miami What was his name the red-headed guy? He came huh? Yeah, whatever the kid but the real not his character David Caruso yeah who was selling furniture after the New York show he was in and then they got him for CSI Miami If you watch CSI Miami, which is a show for old people I mean I get it but still I was is painful because you know I'm like eight sentences ahead of this guy and He it's the same piece if he's saying You know I need a cup of coffee and he's saying oh there's a third dead body It's the same pace it never changed, you know, and you're like really And the Tom Selleck show now The cop shot yeah, I mean it's a little slow, you know, but that's because of who it's for but you kind of know I Know what he's gonna say two sentences, you know before he says it by the way If it if you watch the show at least once to the cop show you should watch it at least once because you grew up with the show and A lot of people don't realize it. It's bonanza Right, that's what it is. It's bonanza. Okay, it's bonanza except it's cops in New York Other than that, it's the same. I think they're using the same scripts. I'm serious I'm convinced they're just taking the bonanza scraps, you know, so I mean he's Lauren green They got the three dumb kids. They got the grandpa The show ends with dinner around the table It's bonanza is what it is. I think they're using the same scripts. That's what I think they're doing You know and it's paced slower though than bonanza was so pace matters Momentum matters Most presentations need to build As they go along by the way the worst pitch people ever who can't pitch well from the front They tend to do the opposite the closer they get to the pitch the slower and more awkward and clumsy and uncomfortable They get right but good ones the pace builds during the course of a presentation You may want to break the presentation up into a series of presentations that are complete They have an open they have a beginning they have a middle they have a bridge to a close They have a close they have some sort of commercial and then they do all that again And sometimes they do it all again Many infomercials are structured that way and by all means lie to them about a lapse time and amount of time coming Yeah, either from the stage or in can presentations I have never told the truth about this in my entire life Okay, I always say there's four minutes and it's nine And in just a few more minutes and it's 20 And there's you know 17 minutes from now you go see and it's 22 Okay, they they have a sense of time but they're not clocking you and And You want to stretch them so if you tell them the truth they start to think about bailing out because 22 minutes is a long time 14 minutes. That's not so much time. So by all means lie Seven things Generally now we talked about sequence of agreements here's seven things that you want in every group presentation They have to agree to these things they have to Realize these things they have to accept these things in order to buy from you So one is appropriateness whatever it is you're selling has to be appropriate for them Hence the presentation to the mailbox shop owners see fails this And nothing else you are going to do Nothing else will save that The authority the credibility the offer the guarantee nothing else is going to save it if it is judged completely inappropriate for me I'm out Differentiation Somehow what you are offering has to be ideally different From every prior attempt they've made at getting the same outcome And rarely are we selling anything to anybody who hasn't had a prior attempt at getting the same outcome so My real estate pitches down the arthritis pitch. I gave you one copy of it. You'll see another one everybody's got glop For pain relief somehow and you and most people Whoever's going to buy glop from me this week for pain relief. They got a drawer full of glop It's all been half used and left in the drawer and they've thrown out another drawer full of glop. They've tried a lot of glop So somehow they got to be convinced there's a difference They are your customers are your buyers are Charlie Brown trying to kick the football Lucy holds They have had it pulled away from them and they've fallen on their ass again and again and again and again and We can make up for it most when we're selling 101 We can make up for it next most when we're in a room and now when you start to get distance It's harder and harder and harder so it you got to convince them It's different if you have to settle for the proverbial new and improved you at least do that But ideally it's different. There has to be some excitement Whatever it is you're selling them has to somehow be cool or exciting to own or fun And they have to get that from your presentation So like if you watch the pocket hose thing The cool factor is It by itself Comes back to you It coils itself back up. That's really cool. You like kind of one-on-one just to watch it do that You know now that's not the best reason to have it, but that's It's cool factor so ideally there's some cool factor to what it is that you are selling and when you're doing Visual selling The reason to do visual selling is to show something So if you don't take advantage of that and you don't show things Then so in many cases and I actually feel this somewhat about some of what we do So you got all the trouble to do the online video launch and the five videos Talking heads for four hours and the thought that always occurs to me I see there was if you just gave me the same number of people who tuned in to watch this and let me send them a sales letter We could skip all this and probably get the same damn result because we don't really have anything to show the purpose of Video is to show shit It's that's its purpose, right? I had a client for some years in Beverly Hills a cosmetic surgeon and He would bring people in to evening seminars to book surgery And he discovered pretty quick that if you had five or six live people there whose breasts you had fixed and made terrific Then when you showed the before picture and then they opened their bosses and let people everybody see it And you could walk up and touch it that was a whole which women do by the way at least I think would come up I mean This was this was much more powerful And it would even be powerful on video instead of him just standing there talking and showing slides You know visual presentations are supposed to be visual They have to know they're not the only idiot So a big problem was selling one to many when nobody sees the many So if we're selling one to many here you see the many and so you start when you come into a room Guy comes into the preview seminar So it's why Pittsburgh went better than Cleveland In part for Bill because he had five times more people at the Pittsburgh seminar that he had at the Cleveland seminar So the rooms flatter People don't like to laugh when there's not that many people in the room But most importantly they come in the room and they think I'm one of a few idiots If they come in the room and there's a bunch more people they say to themselves. They can't all be idiots And so when we do one to many physically This factor can take care of itself if we had For Geyer more recently and when I did success track if we had 35 40 50 docks in the room in San Jose on a Tuesday night. That was enough every doc felt pretty good You might know somebody but at least here's 33 34 35 peers It validates the idea that I came this is okay place to be if he walked in and saw six The sales job suddenly gets infinitely harder Because he thinks I'm one of six morons Oh Well if you're doing one to many but they're watching it on a screen They're the only idiot And so you have to figure out a way to counter that Visually as well as what you say QVC home shopping for example figure out how to counter it by having people call in and By showing the ticker Okay 200 of soul 300 of soul of course you have no way of knowing if that's legit They can start it Just like the piano player put some money in the jar before he starts for the night You never start with an empty jar All right bad idea if you have a restaurant You got a and it's new you got to go rent some cars and put them in the parking lot Because chiropractor office same way just teach them that all time you got to go rent eight ten cars and you got to move them around So nobody ever drives by this place that see so you want to see a really dumb chiropractic office You'll see a brand new office. You'll see a sign new office patients accepted no cars in the parking lot Mary books an appointment. She comes down. She drives in the parking lot. She drives out the parking lot Oh, she says to herself Same thing with a restaurant people drive in the parking lot. It's 6 30 at night. There's only two cars here. Whoops Back out the driveway they go It's the same thing happens with all this right so nobody wants to be the only idiot nobody wants to be the only One getting crippled for life. Nobody wants to be the only person getting food poisoning tonight All right So you got to put some money in the jar before you start playing if you want to get money in the jar You got to understand when you're doing one to many, but they're getting it one on a screen They that's like coming into a room and being one and you sell to them They have to believe they're getting a deal Rich person poor person don't make any difference. They got to somehow believe they're getting a great deal Last they have to be convinced that they're not going to fail. They're not going to be embarrassed They're not going to be frustrated. They're not going to be disappointed again and Finally, they got to believe in reasons to do this Now and again you are handicapped When they can't see people Doing it now, which again is why the QVC thing works So in a physical environment if you know how to start a stampede you can usually start a stampede If you don't know how to stay at start a stampede and you don't have an ethical issue with it You can use shills to start to stampede Shills fake customers You can just see the audience with 8 10 12 people who travel with you from city to city to city Their job is to buy, you know, they sit there They take notes and they're the first ones who jump up and run to the back of the room very excitedly don't think people don't do it You can create quasi shills, you know, so you can Pick up you can make sure you've got really good customers in the room who buy everything along with the prospects you can Coupons some people in advance there's things you can do if you're not particularly good at creating a stampede But however you get it done it does feed on itself as soon as some people start moving to buy other people buy At that state fair at the county fair in the tent the guy still doing the knives As soon as one or two people buy People from where who were standing way at the back during the demo because they didn't want to get called on and they didn't want to Get pressed to buy they start moving forward and buy Last time when I was a Disney For the food show during Carlos free class that cost me a fortune Before before I bought all the balls. I never actually even got out of the expo hall so the cut-go knife guy was there and And doing you know the demo and they all know by the way how to cut that to me so it doesn't It's a skill My guess is they could do it with their thumb Well, I don't think it has anything to do with the knife, right? But but so the cut-go knife is guys there So the demo, you know, he has a conversation with you talks down Probably takes 20 minutes for him to do everything so while he is doing that to me which I've predetermined I'm gonna buy by the way, it's just but now people stop right and they're like behind and around me Kind of watching but kind of not wanting, you know to be reached out and Grabbed so I'm a good enthusiastic buyer, right? So I'm like buying excitedly and I'm buying two sets Sonnet they're like 800 bucks. I think so I got like 1600 bucks worth of knives Carla didn't see those they ship them. So all she saw was the pile of $200 balls. That was bad enough, but So but the people who have gathered around they all bought I Got everybody so after they all leave I says, you know, you should give me those for free Yeah, he says no, you're you're right. He said you're absolutely right He said the attitude of the first one to buy Controls the closing percentage of the percentage that are there to buy right so we can make that happen in a physical environment Now Charlie Brown is watching you on a screen He's home alone He doesn't see it So it's good for us to make sure he knows it's happening QVC does it with the clicker And there's only so many left and there's been somebody sold and there's and you'll notice if you watch QVC at all You will notice every single time by the way, there's momentum You will never see something on QVC where There's a big rush of sales when they first start and then it trickles down Doesn't happen. It's always the other way. There's a momentum So we do it with webcasts by every time we come back from a break recognizing Bill Brown from Tampa and Susie from the UK and Somebody with some unpronounceable name with all vowels in it from somewhere and so we Do the you know the romper room? Barbers with us this morning and Charlie's with us this morning. So we're not doing that to give Pat on the head to the buyer. We're doing it as demonstration That people are buying That's what we're doing. So you got to think about this issue now the big one Still in the middle the mandatory explanation the question you've got to answer That people care I am going to show Phil That people care deeply about That has to be taken care of in the middle before they can buy from you That nobody should care about at all Is why you are selling to them other than to make money? Now nobody should ever care about it The knives are either good or bad All right, and obviously the guy's there to make money because he rented space and he's standing there and he's selling knives You can't deny he's there to make money But people deeply care for no good reason If I need heart surgery I should want the best heart surgeon in the world and If he marches it if his office is decorated like this I go into his office for the consult and there's Money on the wall and there's gold dollar signs and there's a pirate flag I Shouldn't give a shit What do I care? I? Only care I got the best heart guy I can get but that's not how people's heads work It just isn't the way their heads work All right, they want there to be a mission a Reason in addition to other than the money So they want you selling this health product because Your sister died from this condition They want you in the fire alarm business because of some reason other than Their eight grand and I make three grand every time I sell a set All right now again, they shouldn't care They should only care I'm getting good fire alarms and now my dog won't you know be the lone survivor That's all they should care about But people care about the other thing So somewhere in the middle of your presentation Needs to be a reason you are doing what you're doing that does not directly have to do With the money you are making If you go through my Peter Low presentation carefully and you listen to most other times when I sell I Will if it's generic I will sort of introduce the Dan loves entrepreneurs and small business people And I grew up with my parents as small business people and we are the backbone of America I will be the champion of the small business guy. I am selling to I'm selling to chiropractors It's because I love chiropractors work with chiropractors my entire life I've always had back trouble and if it wasn't for chiropractors I wouldn't be standing here in front of you today Now they shouldn't give a rat's ass about that If I got a box of stuff that is going to flood their office with patients It shouldn't matter them if I never go to a chiropractor.
